summary: Comprehensive Java Interview Questions covering Core Java, OOPS Concepts, JVM/JDK/JRE, String Handling, Exception Handling, Collections Framework, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction, Encapsulation, Multithreading, and commonly asked Java concepts for QA Engineers, Automation Testers, and SDETs.

content.heading: What is Java?
content.body: Java is an object-oriented, platform-independent programming language that follows the principle "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA).

content.heading: What are the features of Java?
content.body: Java is platform-independent, object-oriented, robust, secure, multithreaded, portable, and provides automatic memory management through Garbage Collection.

content.heading: What is JVM?
content.body: JVM (Java Virtual Machine) executes Java bytecode and enables Java programs to run on different operating systems.

content.heading: What is JRE?
content.body: JRE (Java Runtime Environment) contains JVM and supporting libraries required to run Java applications.

content.heading: What is JDK?
content.body: JDK (Java Development Kit) contains JRE along with development tools such as javac, debugger, and compiler utilities.

content.heading: What is the relationship between JVM, JRE, and JDK?
content.body: JVM executes bytecode, JRE provides the runtime environment, and JDK provides development tools along with JRE.

content.heading: What are Primitive Data Types in Java?
content.body: Java provides eight primitive data types: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, and boolean.

content.heading: What is a Wrapper Class?
content.body: Wrapper Classes convert primitive data types into objects. Example: int → Integer, char → Character.

content.heading: What is the String Pool?
content.body: String Pool is a special memory area inside Heap memory where string literals are stored and reused to avoid duplicate object creation.

content.heading: What is the difference between == and .equals()?
content.body: == compares memory references, whereas .equals() compares actual object values or content.

content.heading: What is String in Java?
content.body: String is an immutable class used to represent character sequences. Once created, its value cannot be modified.

content.heading: What is StringBuilder?
content.body: StringBuilder is a mutable class used for efficient string manipulation and is not thread-safe.

content.heading: What is StringBuffer?
content.body: StringBuffer is a mutable and thread-safe class used for string manipulation in multithreaded environments.

content.heading: What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
content.body: String is immutable, StringBuilder is mutable and faster, while StringBuffer is mutable and thread-safe.

content.heading: What is a Constructor?
content.body: A Constructor is a special method used to initialize objects and has the same name as the class.

content.heading: What are the types of Constructors?
content.body: Default Constructor, Parameterized Constructor, and Copy Constructor (conceptual).

content.heading: What is a Default Constructor?
content.body: A Default Constructor is automatically provided by Java if no constructor is explicitly defined.

content.heading: What is a Parameterized Constructor?
content.body: A Parameterized Constructor accepts arguments during object creation.

content.heading: What is an Object?
content.body: An Object is an instance of a class and represents a real-world entity with state and behavior.

content.heading: What is a Class?
content.body: A Class is a blueprint or template used to create objects by defining data members and methods.

content.heading: What is OOPS?
content.body: OOPS (Object-Oriented Programming System) is a programming approach based on objects that combines data and behavior together. It improves code reusability, maintainability, and scalability.

content.heading: What are the main concepts of OOPS?
content.body: The four main OOPS concepts are Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction.

content.heading: What is Encapsulation?
content.body: Encapsulation is the process of wrapping data and methods together into a single unit (class) and restricting direct access using access modifiers.

content.heading: What are the benefits of Encapsulation?
content.body: Data hiding, better security, maintainability, flexibility, and controlled access to data.

content.heading: What is Abstraction?
content.body: Abstraction is the process of hiding implementation details and showing only essential functionality to the user.

content.heading: What are the benefits of Abstraction?
content.body: Reduces complexity, improves maintainability, and allows developers to focus on functionality rather than implementation details.

content.heading: What is Inheritance?
content.body: Inheritance allows one class to acquire properties and methods from another class using the extends keyword.

content.heading: What are the advantages of Inheritance?
content.body: Code reusability, reduced duplication, easier maintenance, and hierarchical relationships between classes.

content.heading: What is Polymorphism?
content.body: Polymorphism allows a single functionality to behave differently based on context.

content.heading: What are the types of Polymorphism?
content.body: Compile-Time Polymorphism (Method Overloading) and Runtime Polymorphism (Method Overriding).

content.heading: What is Method Overloading?
content.body: Method Overloading occurs when multiple methods have the same name but different parameters within the same class.
content.code: void login(String user){} void login(String user,String pwd){}

content.heading: What is Method Overriding?
content.body: Method Overriding occurs when a child class provides its own implementation of a parent class method with the same signature.

content.heading: What is the difference between Overloading and Overriding?
content.body: Overloading uses different parameters and is resolved at compile time, whereas Overriding uses the same method signature and is resolved at runtime.

content.heading: What is Compile-Time Polymorphism?
content.body: Compile-Time Polymorphism is achieved through Method Overloading where method binding occurs during compilation.

content.heading: What is Runtime Polymorphism?
content.body: Runtime Polymorphism is achieved through Method Overriding where method binding occurs during execution.

content.heading: What is an Abstract Class?
content.body: An Abstract Class is a class that cannot be instantiated and may contain both abstract and concrete methods.
content.code: abstract class Employee { abstract void work(); }

content.heading: What is an Interface?
content.body: An Interface defines a contract that classes must implement. It is used to achieve abstraction and multiple inheritance.

content.heading: What is the difference between Abstract Class and Interface?
content.body: Abstract Classes can contain both abstract and concrete methods, whereas Interfaces primarily define behavior contracts.

content.heading: Can an Interface have variables?
content.body: Yes. Interface variables are implicitly public, static, and final.

content.heading: Can an Abstract Class have a constructor?
content.body: Yes. Abstract classes can contain constructors which are executed when child objects are created.

content.heading: What is Method Hiding?
content.body: Method Hiding occurs when a child class defines a static method with the same signature as a static method in the parent class.

content.heading: What is the final keyword?
content.body: final is used to restrict modification. It can be applied to variables, methods, and classes.

content.heading: What is a final variable?
content.body: A final variable cannot be reassigned once initialized.

content.heading: What is a final method?
content.body: A final method cannot be overridden by child classes.

content.heading: What is a final class?
content.body: A final class cannot be extended or inherited.

content.heading: What is finally block?
content.body: finally is a block that executes whether an exception occurs or not. It is commonly used for resource cleanup.

content.heading: What is finalize() method?
content.body: finalize() is a method that was historically called by Garbage Collector before object destruction. It is deprecated in modern Java versions.

content.heading: What is the difference between final, finally, and finalize()?
content.body: final is a keyword, finally is an exception handling block, and finalize() is a garbage collection related method.

content.heading: What are Access Modifiers in Java?
content.body: Access Modifiers control visibility of classes, methods, and variables. They include public, private, protected, and default.

content.heading: What is the scope of Access Modifiers?
content.body: public → everywhere, private → same class, protected → package + subclass, default → same package.

content.heading: What is an Exception?
content.body: An Exception is an event that occurs during program execution and disrupts the normal flow of the program. It is typically caused by runtime errors.

content.heading: What are examples of Exceptions?
content.body: Common examples include Divide by Zero, File Not Found, Null Pointer Access, Invalid Array Index, and Database Connection Failures.

content.heading: What is Exception Handling?
content.body: Exception Handling is a mechanism used to handle runtime errors gracefully without terminating the program unexpectedly.

content.heading: Why is Exception Handling important?
content.body: It prevents application crashes, improves stability, enables graceful error recovery, and provides meaningful error messages.

content.heading: What are the keywords used in Exception Handling?
content.body: try, catch, finally, throw, and throws.

content.heading: What is a try block?
content.body: The try block contains code that may generate an exception and is monitored during execution.

content.heading: What is a catch block?
content.body: The catch block handles exceptions thrown by the try block.

content.heading: What is a finally block?
content.body: The finally block executes regardless of whether an exception occurs or not and is commonly used for cleanup activities.

content.heading: When is finally commonly used?
content.body: Closing browsers, closing database connections, releasing resources, and cleanup activities.

content.heading: What is the throw keyword?
content.body: throw is used to explicitly create and throw an exception within a method or block.

content.heading: What is the throws keyword?
content.body: throws is used in method signatures to declare exceptions that may be thrown by the method.

content.heading: What is the difference between throw and throws?
content.body: throw is used to explicitly throw an exception, whereas throws declares possible exceptions in a method signature.

content.heading: What is a Checked Exception?
content.body: Checked Exceptions are verified at compile time and must be handled or declared using throws.

content.heading: Give examples of Checked Exceptions.
content.body: IOException, FileNotFoundException, SQLException, and ClassNotFoundException.

content.heading: What is an Unchecked Exception?
content.body: Unchecked Exceptions occur at runtime and are not checked by the compiler.

content.heading: Give examples of Unchecked Exceptions.
content.body: NullPointerException, ArithmeticException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, and NumberFormatException.

content.heading: What is NullPointerException?
content.body: NullPointerException occurs when an operation is performed on a null object reference.

content.heading: What is ArithmeticException?
content.body: ArithmeticException occurs when illegal arithmetic operations are performed, such as division by zero.

content.heading: What is ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException?
content.body: This exception occurs when attempting to access an array index that does not exist.

content.heading: What is NumberFormatException?
content.body: This exception occurs when converting invalid strings into numeric values.

content.heading: What is FileNotFoundException?
content.body: FileNotFoundException occurs when the specified file cannot be located during file operations.

content.heading: What is SQLException?
content.body: SQLException occurs when database access errors happen during SQL operations.

content.heading: What is Exception Hierarchy in Java?
content.body: All exceptions inherit from Throwable, which is divided into Error and Exception categories.

content.heading: What is the difference between Error and Exception?
content.body: Errors represent serious system issues that applications typically cannot recover from, while Exceptions represent conditions that applications can handle.

content.heading: Can we have multiple catch blocks?
content.body: Yes, multiple catch blocks can be used to handle different exception types separately.

content.heading: What is Multi-Catch in Java?
content.body: Multi-Catch allows handling multiple exception types within a single catch block.

content.heading: What happens if an exception is not handled?
content.body: The JVM terminates the program and prints the exception stack trace.

content.heading: What is a Custom Exception?
content.body: A Custom Exception is a user-defined exception class created by extending Exception or RuntimeException.

content.heading: Why are Custom Exceptions used?
content.body: Custom Exceptions improve readability, business rule validation, and provide meaningful error handling specific to application requirements.

content.heading: What are Exception Handling Best Practices?
content.body: Catch specific exceptions, avoid empty catch blocks, use finally for cleanup, create meaningful messages, log exceptions properly, and avoid catching generic Exception unnecessarily.

content.heading: What is Collection Framework in Java?
content.body: Collection Framework is a set of classes and interfaces used to store, manipulate, and process groups of objects dynamically.

content.heading: What are the main interfaces in Collection Framework?
content.body: Collection and Map are the two primary interfaces. Collection further includes List, Set, and Queue.

content.heading: What is the Collection Interface?
content.body: Collection is the root interface for List, Set, and Queue implementations.

content.heading: What is List?
content.body: List is an ordered collection that allows duplicate elements and maintains insertion order.

content.heading: What is Set?
content.body: Set is a collection that does not allow duplicate elements.

content.heading: What is Map?
content.body: Map stores data in key-value pairs where keys are unique.

content.heading: What is Queue?
content.body: Queue follows FIFO (First In First Out) ordering and is used for processing elements sequentially.

content.heading: What is ArrayList?
content.body: ArrayList is a dynamic array implementation of List that allows duplicates and maintains insertion order.

content.heading: What are the advantages of ArrayList?
content.body: Fast retrieval, dynamic sizing, maintains insertion order, and easy traversal.

content.heading: What is LinkedList?
content.body: LinkedList is a doubly linked list implementation that provides efficient insertion and deletion operations.

content.heading: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?
content.body: ArrayList provides faster retrieval, whereas LinkedList provides faster insertion and deletion.

content.heading: What is Vector?
content.body: Vector is a thread-safe dynamic array implementation.

content.heading: What is Stack?
content.body: Stack follows the LIFO (Last In First Out) principle.

content.heading: What are push() and pop() methods?
content.body: push() adds elements to the stack and pop() removes the top element from the stack.

content.heading: What is HashSet?
content.body: HashSet is an unordered collection that does not allow duplicate elements and uses hashing internally.

content.heading: What is LinkedHashSet?
content.body: LinkedHashSet maintains insertion order while preventing duplicate values.

content.heading: What is TreeSet?
content.body: TreeSet stores unique elements in sorted order using a tree structure.

content.heading: What is the difference between HashSet and TreeSet?
content.body: HashSet is unordered and faster, whereas TreeSet stores elements in sorted order but is relatively slower.

content.heading: What is HashMap?
content.body: HashMap stores key-value pairs, allows one null key, multiple null values, and is not thread-safe.

content.heading: What is LinkedHashMap?
content.body: LinkedHashMap maintains insertion order while storing key-value pairs.

content.heading: What is TreeMap?
content.body: TreeMap stores key-value pairs in sorted order based on keys.

content.heading: What is Hashtable?
content.body: Hashtable is a thread-safe implementation of Map and does not allow null keys or values.

content.heading: What is ConcurrentHashMap?
content.body: ConcurrentHashMap is a thread-safe, high-performance implementation designed for concurrent environments.

content.heading: What is the difference between HashMap and Hashtable?
content.body: HashMap is not thread-safe and allows null values, whereas Hashtable is thread-safe and does not allow null keys or values.

content.heading: What is the difference between HashMap and TreeMap?
content.body: HashMap is unordered and faster, whereas TreeMap stores keys in sorted order using a Red-Black Tree.

content.heading: What is Iterator?
content.body: Iterator is used to traverse collection elements one by one.

content.heading: What are the methods available in Iterator?
content.body: hasNext(), next(), and remove() are the primary Iterator methods.

content.heading: What is Comparable?
content.body: Comparable provides natural sorting and uses the compareTo() method.
content.code: public int compareTo(Employee e){ return this.id-e.id; }

content.heading: What is Comparator?
content.body: Comparator provides custom sorting logic using the compare() method.
content.code: public int compare(Employee a, Employee b){ return a.id-b.id; }

content.heading: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator?
content.body: Comparable performs natural sorting within the class, whereas Comparator provides custom sorting outside the class.

content.heading: What is PriorityQueue?
content.body: PriorityQueue orders elements according to their priority rather than insertion order.

content.heading: What is ArrayDeque?
content.body: ArrayDeque is a faster alternative to Stack and LinkedList for queue operations.

content.heading: Which Collection allows duplicates?
content.body: List implementations such as ArrayList, LinkedList, and Vector allow duplicates.

content.heading: Which Collection does not allow duplicates?
content.body: Set implementations such as HashSet, LinkedHashSet, and TreeSet do not allow duplicates.

content.heading: Which Collection maintains insertion order?
content.body: ArrayList, LinkedList, LinkedHashSet, and LinkedHashMap maintain insertion order.

content.heading: Which Collection stores data in sorted order?
content.body: TreeSet and TreeMap automatically store data in sorted order.

content.heading: Which Collection is thread-safe?
content.body: Vector, Hashtable, and ConcurrentHashMap provide thread-safe implementations.

content.heading: When would you use ArrayList over LinkedList?
content.body: When frequent retrieval operations are required and insertions/deletions are relatively infrequent.

content.heading: When would you use LinkedList over ArrayList?
content.body: When frequent insertions and deletions occur in the middle of the collection.

content.heading: What are Collection Framework Best Practices?
content.body: Use interfaces instead of implementations, choose collections based on requirements, avoid unnecessary synchronization, and prefer Concurrent Collections for multithreading.

content.heading: What is a Thread in Java?
content.body: A Thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads can run concurrently within the same application.

content.heading: What is Multithreading?
content.body: Multithreading is the process of executing multiple threads simultaneously to improve performance and resource utilization.

content.heading: What are the advantages of Multithreading?
content.body: Improved performance, better CPU utilization, faster execution, concurrent task processing, and responsive applications.

content.heading: How do you create a Thread in Java?
content.body: Threads can be created by extending the Thread class or implementing the Runnable interface.
content.code: class MyThread extends Thread { public void run(){} }

content.heading: How do you create a Thread using Runnable?
content.body: Implement the Runnable interface and pass the object to a Thread instance.

content.heading: What is the difference between Thread and Runnable?
content.body: Thread is a class whereas Runnable is an interface. Runnable is preferred because Java supports single inheritance.

content.heading: What are the stages in Thread Lifecycle?
content.body: New, Runnable, Running, Blocked/Waiting, and Terminated.

content.heading: What is Synchronization?
content.body: Synchronization controls access to shared resources and prevents multiple threads from modifying data simultaneously.

content.heading: Why is Synchronization required?
content.body: To prevent race conditions, data inconsistency, and thread interference in concurrent applications.

content.heading: What is synchronized keyword?
content.body: synchronized ensures only one thread can access a synchronized block or method at a time.

content.heading: What is a synchronized block?
content.body: A synchronized block restricts access to a specific code section rather than the entire method.

content.heading: What is Deadlock?
content.body: Deadlock occurs when two or more threads wait indefinitely for resources held by each other.

content.heading: How can Deadlock be avoided?
content.body: Avoid nested locks, maintain lock ordering, minimize lock scope, and use concurrent utilities.

content.heading: What is volatile keyword?
content.body: volatile ensures variable updates are immediately visible to all threads.

content.heading: What is Executor Framework?
content.body: Executor Framework provides a higher-level API for managing and executing threads efficiently.

content.heading: How do you create a Thread Pool?
content.body: Use Executors utility class to create fixed, cached, or scheduled thread pools.

content.heading: What is Callable Interface?
content.body: Callable is similar to Runnable but can return a value and throw checked exceptions.

content.heading: What is Future?
content.body: Future represents the result of an asynchronous computation.

content.heading: What is a Lambda Expression?
content.body: Lambda Expressions provide a concise way to represent anonymous functions introduced in Java 8.

content.heading: What are the advantages of Lambda Expressions?
content.body: Reduced code, improved readability, support for functional programming, and easier collection processing.

content.heading: What is a Functional Interface?
content.body: A Functional Interface contains exactly one abstract method and can be implemented using Lambda Expressions.

content.heading: Give examples of Functional Interfaces.
content.body: Runnable, Callable, Comparator, Consumer, Supplier, Predicate, and Function.

content.heading: What is Stream API?
content.body: Stream API processes collections using functional operations such as filter, map, reduce, and collect.

content.heading: What is filter() in Stream API?
content.body: filter() selects elements matching a specified condition.

content.heading: What is map() in Stream API?
content.body: map() transforms elements from one form into another.

content.heading: What is collect() in Stream API?
content.body: collect() gathers processed stream elements into collections or other data structures.

content.heading: What are the major Java 8 features?
content.body: Lambda Expressions, Stream API, Functional Interfaces, Method References, Optional Class, Default Methods, and Date-Time API.

content.heading: What is Garbage Collection?
content.body: Garbage Collection is the automatic process of reclaiming memory occupied by unused objects.

content.heading: How does Garbage Collection work?
content.body: JVM identifies unreachable objects using algorithms such as Mark-and-Sweep and removes them from memory.

content.heading: What is the role of JVM in Memory Management?
content.body: JVM manages Heap Memory, Stack Memory, Garbage Collection, Object Allocation, and Runtime Execution of Java applications.

title: Cucumber BDD Interview Questions and Answers
summary: Comprehensive Cucumber BDD Interview Questions covering Gherkin Syntax, Feature Files, Step Definitions, Hooks, Tags, Scenario Outline, Data Tables, Test Runner Configuration, and Cucumber Framework Concepts.

content.heading: What is Cucumber?
content.body: Cucumber is a Behavior Driven Development (BDD) tool used to write test cases in plain English using Gherkin language. It helps bridge communication between business and technical teams.

content.heading: What is BDD?
content.body: Behavior Driven Development (BDD) is a software development approach that focuses on application behavior from the end-user perspective using business-readable scenarios.

content.heading: What is Gherkin?
content.body: Gherkin is a business-readable language used to write Feature Files in Cucumber.

content.heading: What are the main keywords in Gherkin?
content.body: Feature, Scenario, Scenario Outline, Given, When, Then, And, But, Examples, and Background.

content.heading: What is a Feature File?
content.body: A Feature File is a file with a .feature extension that contains scenarios written in Gherkin language.

content.heading: What is the purpose of a Feature File?
content.body: Feature Files describe application behavior in a format understandable by business users, developers, and testers.

content.heading: What is a Scenario in Cucumber?
content.body: A Scenario represents a single test case that validates a specific business functionality.

content.heading: What is a Scenario Outline?
content.body: Scenario Outline is used to execute the same scenario multiple times with different sets of test data.

content.heading: Which keyword is used with Scenario Outline?
content.body: The Examples keyword is used to provide multiple datasets for Scenario Outline execution.

content.heading: What is the difference between Scenario and Scenario Outline?
content.body: Scenario executes once with a single dataset, whereas Scenario Outline executes multiple times using data from the Examples table.

content.heading: What is Given in Gherkin?
content.body: Given defines the precondition or initial state of the application before an action is performed.

content.heading: What is When in Gherkin?
content.body: When defines the action performed by the user or system.

content.heading: What is Then in Gherkin?
content.body: Then defines the expected outcome or result after performing an action.

content.heading: What is And keyword?
content.body: And is used to combine multiple Given, When, or Then statements for better readability.

content.heading: What is But keyword?
content.body: But is used to define negative conditions or exceptions in a scenario.

content.heading: What is a Step Definition?
content.body: Step Definitions are Java methods that map Gherkin steps to automation code using annotations such as @Given, @When, and @Then.

content.heading: Which annotations are used in Step Definitions?
content.body: @Given, @When, @Then, @And, and @But.

content.heading: What is Background in Cucumber?
content.body: Background contains steps that execute before every scenario within a Feature File.

content.heading: Why is Background used?
content.body: Background helps avoid duplication of common setup steps across multiple scenarios.

content.heading: What are Hooks in Cucumber?
content.body: Hooks are special blocks of code that execute before or after scenarios.

content.heading: Which Hooks are commonly used in Cucumber?
content.body: @Before and @After are the most commonly used hooks.

content.heading: What is @Before Hook?
content.body: @Before executes before each scenario and is commonly used for browser initialization and test setup.

content.heading: What is @After Hook?
content.body: @After executes after each scenario and is commonly used for cleanup activities and closing resources.

content.heading: How do you pass data in Cucumber?
content.body: Data can be passed using Scenario Outline with Examples, DataTable, or DocString.

content.heading: What is DataTable in Cucumber?
content.body: DataTable is used to pass structured tabular data from Feature Files to Step Definitions.

content.heading: What is DocString in Cucumber?
content.body: DocString is used to pass large blocks of text or JSON/XML payloads into Step Definitions.

content.heading: What is the pipe (
content.body: ) operator used for?
content.codeType: The pipe operator is used to define structured test data within a DataTable.

content.heading: What is a Test Runner in Cucumber?
content.body: The Test Runner class connects Feature Files and Step Definitions and controls test execution.

content.heading: What is @CucumberOptions?
content.body: @CucumberOptions is used to configure feature paths, glue code, reports, tags, and execution settings.

content.heading: What is the purpose of the glue option?
content.body: The glue option specifies the package location containing Step Definition classes.

content.heading: What is the purpose of the features option?
content.body: The features option specifies the location of Feature Files.

content.heading: What is the purpose of plugin option?
content.body: The plugin option configures reporting formats such as HTML, JSON, and Pretty reports.

content.heading: What are Tags in Cucumber?
content.body: Tags are labels used to group, organize, and selectively execute scenarios.

content.heading: Why are Tags used?
content.body: Tags help execute specific subsets of scenarios such as Smoke, Regression, or Sanity tests.

content.heading: Give examples of commonly used Tags.
content.body: @Smoke, @Regression, @Sanity, @API, @UI, @Critical.

content.heading: How do you execute only Smoke tests?
content.body: Configure the Test Runner using the tags option.

content.heading: What is the benefit of using Cucumber?
content.body: Cucumber improves collaboration, provides readable test cases, supports automation, and promotes shared understanding among teams.

content.heading: What is the relationship between Feature Files and Step Definitions?
content.body: Feature Files define behavior in Gherkin, while Step Definitions contain the automation code that executes those steps.

content.heading: How does Cucumber support Automation Testing?
content.body: Cucumber connects business-readable scenarios to automation code, enabling automated execution of requirements.

content.heading: What are Cucumber Best Practices?
content.body: Keep Feature Files business-focused, reuse Step Definitions, use meaningful tags, avoid duplicate steps, and maintain readable scenarios.

title: TestNG Interview Questions and Answers
summary: Comprehensive TestNG Interview Questions covering Annotations, Assertions, TestNG XML, Groups, Priorities, Dependencies, DataProvider, Listeners, Parallel Execution, and Framework Integration.

content.heading: What is TestNG?
content.body: TestNG is a testing framework inspired by JUnit and NUnit that provides advanced features such as annotations, grouping, parallel execution, reporting, and data-driven testing.

content.heading: Why is TestNG used?
content.body: TestNG helps organize test execution, supports parallel testing, data-driven testing, dependency management, and detailed reporting.

content.heading: What are the advantages of TestNG?
content.body: Annotations, Prioritization, Grouping, Parallel Execution, DataProvider, Listeners, Dependency Management, and Reporting.

content.heading: What are TestNG Annotations?
content.body: Annotations control the execution flow of test methods and configuration methods.

content.heading: What is @Test annotation?
content.body: @Test identifies a method as a test case that should be executed by TestNG.

content.heading: What is @BeforeSuite?
content.body: Executes once before all tests in the suite.

content.heading: What is @AfterSuite?
content.body: Executes once after all tests in the suite complete.

content.heading: What is @BeforeTest?
content.body: Executes before any test methods belonging to a test tag in testng.xml.

content.heading: What is @AfterTest?
content.body: Executes after all test methods belonging to a test tag complete.

content.heading: What is @BeforeClass?
content.body: Executes once before the first test method in a class.

content.heading: What is @AfterClass?
content.body: Executes once after all test methods in a class finish.

content.heading: What is @BeforeMethod?
content.body: Executes before each test method.

content.heading: What is @AfterMethod?
content.body: Executes after each test method.

content.heading: What is the execution order of TestNG annotations?
content.body: @BeforeSuite → @BeforeTest → @BeforeClass → @BeforeMethod → @Test → @AfterMethod → @AfterClass → @AfterTest → @AfterSuite

content.heading: What is testng.xml?
content.body: testng.xml is a configuration file used to control test execution, suites, groups, parameters, and parallel execution.

content.heading: Why is testng.xml important?
content.body: It allows execution control without modifying test code.

content.heading: What is Priority in TestNG?
content.body: Priority controls the execution order of test methods. Lower numbers execute first.

content.heading: What happens if priority is not specified?
content.body: TestNG executes methods alphabetically by default.

content.heading: What is enabled=false?
content.body: It skips execution of a test method.

content.heading: What is invocationCount?
content.body: invocationCount executes the same test multiple times.

content.heading: What are Assertions in TestNG?
content.body: Assertions are validation points used to compare expected and actual results during test execution.

content.heading: Why are Assertions important?
content.body: Assertions determine whether a test case passes or fails based on validation results.

content.heading: What is Hard Assert?
content.body: Hard Assert immediately stops test execution when an assertion fails.

content.heading: What is Soft Assert?
content.body: Soft Assert continues execution even if validation fails and reports failures at the end using assertAll().

content.heading: What is the difference between Hard Assert and Soft Assert?
content.body: Hard Assert stops execution on failure, whereas Soft Assert collects failures and continues execution until assertAll() is called.

content.heading: What is assertEquals()?
content.body: assertEquals() validates whether expected and actual values are equal.

content.heading: What is assertTrue()?
content.body: assertTrue() verifies that a condition evaluates to true.

content.heading: What is assertFalse()?
content.body: assertFalse() verifies that a condition evaluates to false.

content.heading: What is assertNull()?
content.body: assertNull() validates that an object reference is null.

content.heading: What is assertNotNull()?
content.body: assertNotNull() validates that an object reference is not null.

content.heading: What is a Group in TestNG?
content.body: Groups are used to organize and execute related test cases together.

content.heading: Why are Groups used?
content.body: Groups help execute specific categories such as Smoke, Regression, Sanity, or API tests.

content.heading: How do you define a Group?
content.body: Groups are defined using the groups attribute inside the @Test annotation.

content.heading: How do you execute a specific Group?
content.body: Use testng.xml and include the required group under the groups section.

content.heading: Can a Test belong to multiple Groups?
content.body: Yes, a test can belong to multiple groups simultaneously.

content.heading: What is dependsOnMethods?
content.body: dependsOnMethods ensures a test executes only after specified methods complete successfully.

content.heading: Why is dependsOnMethods used?
content.body: It helps maintain execution flow for dependent business scenarios.

content.heading: What happens if a dependent method fails?
content.body: The dependent test is skipped automatically.

content.heading: What is dependsOnGroups?
content.body: dependsOnGroups allows a test to depend on the successful execution of one or more groups.

content.heading: What is Parameterization in TestNG?
content.body: Parameterization allows passing external values into test methods during execution.

content.heading: What is @Parameters annotation?
content.body: @Parameters reads values from testng.xml and passes them into test methods.

content.heading: What is DataProvider?
content.body: DataProvider is a TestNG feature used to execute the same test multiple times with different datasets.

content.heading: Why is DataProvider used?
content.body: It supports data-driven testing without duplicating test scripts.

content.heading: How do you create a DataProvider?
content.body: DataProvider methods return Object[][] containing test data.

content.heading: How do you use DataProvider in a Test?
content.body: Reference the DataProvider name within the @Test annotation.

content.heading: What is the return type of DataProvider?
content.body: Typically Object[][] or Iterator<Object[]>.

content.heading: What is SkipException?
content.body: SkipException is used to skip a test dynamically during execution.

content.heading: What is expectedExceptions in TestNG?
content.body: expectedExceptions validates that a specific exception is thrown during execution.

content.heading: What is timeout in TestNG?
content.body: timeout defines the maximum execution time allowed for a test method.

content.heading: What are the benefits of Data-Driven Testing in TestNG?
content.body: Increased coverage, reduced code duplication, improved maintainability, and support for multiple datasets using a single test script.

content.heading: What are Listeners in TestNG?
content.body: Listeners are interfaces that allow developers to monitor and customize test execution events.

content.heading: Why are Listeners used?
content.body: Listeners help capture execution events, generate reports, take screenshots, log results, and customize framework behavior.

content.heading: What is ITestListener?
content.body: ITestListener is a TestNG interface used to listen to test execution events such as pass, fail, skip, and start.

content.heading: What are the commonly used methods in ITestListener?
content.body: onTestStart(), onTestSuccess(), onTestFailure(), onTestSkipped(), and onFinish().

content.heading: What is onTestFailure()?
content.body: onTestFailure() executes automatically when a test case fails. It is commonly used for screenshots and logging.

content.heading: What is ISuiteListener?
content.body: ISuiteListener listens to suite-level execution events before and after suite execution.

content.heading: What is IReporter?
content.body: IReporter is used to generate customized execution reports after test completion.

content.heading: How do you register a Listener?
content.body: Listeners can be registered using the @Listeners annotation or through testng.xml.

content.heading: What is Retry Analyzer?
content.body: Retry Analyzer automatically reruns failed test cases based on configured retry logic.

content.heading: Why is Retry Analyzer used?
content.body: It helps handle flaky tests caused by temporary environment or synchronization issues.

content.heading: Which interface is used for Retry Analyzer?
content.body: IRetryAnalyzer interface is used to implement retry logic.

content.heading: How do you attach Retry Analyzer to a Test?
content.body: Use the retryAnalyzer attribute inside the @Test annotation.

content.heading: What are TestNG Reports?
content.body: TestNG automatically generates execution reports that show passed, failed, skipped tests, and execution statistics.

content.heading: Which default reports are generated by TestNG?
content.body: emailable-report.html and index.html.

content.heading: What information is available in TestNG Reports?
content.body: Total tests executed, passed tests, failed tests, skipped tests, execution time, and stack traces.

content.heading: What is Extent Report?
content.body: Extent Report is a third-party reporting library that provides rich HTML execution reports with logs and screenshots.

content.heading: Why are Extent Reports popular?
content.body: They provide visually appealing reports with screenshots, categories, logs, and detailed execution history.

content.heading: What is Allure Report?
content.body: Allure is an advanced reporting framework that provides detailed execution reports, trends, and test history.

content.heading: What is Parallel Execution in TestNG?
content.body: Parallel Execution allows multiple test cases to run simultaneously to reduce execution time.

content.heading: Why is Parallel Execution important?
content.body: It significantly reduces regression execution time and improves resource utilization.

content.heading: How do you enable Parallel Execution?
content.body: Configure parallel attributes in testng.xml.

content.heading: What is thread-count?
content.body: thread-count defines the maximum number of parallel threads used during execution.

content.heading: What are the parallel execution modes in TestNG?
content.body: methods, classes, tests, and instances.

content.heading: What is parallel="methods"?
content.body: Executes test methods concurrently.

content.heading: What is parallel="classes"?
content.body: Executes classes concurrently.

content.heading: What is parallel="tests"?
content.body: Executes test tags in parallel.

content.heading: What is Cross Browser Execution?
content.body: Running the same automation suite across multiple browsers such as Chrome, Firefox, and Edge.

content.heading: How is Cross Browser Execution achieved in TestNG?
content.body: Through Parameters, DataProviders, or Selenium Grid integration.

content.heading: What is Selenium Grid?
content.body: Selenium Grid enables distributed execution across multiple machines, browsers, and operating systems.

content.heading: What are TestNG Best Practices?
content.body: Use proper annotations, keep tests independent, implement retry logic carefully, use DataProviders for test data, integrate reporting, and leverage parallel execution for faster feedback.

content.heading: What is Factory Annotation in TestNG?
content.body: @Factory is used to create multiple instances of test classes dynamically.

content.heading: What is @Listeners Annotation?
content.body: @Listeners registers listener classes directly within test classes.

content.heading: What is alwaysRun=true?
content.body: alwaysRun ensures a method executes even if dependent methods fail.

content.heading: What is preserve-order in testng.xml?
content.body: preserve-order maintains execution order defined in the XML file.

content.heading: How do you execute failed test cases only?
content.body: TestNG generates testng-failed.xml which can be executed separately.

content.heading: What is testng-failed.xml?
content.body: An automatically generated file containing only failed and skipped test cases from the previous execution.

content.heading: How do you parameterize browsers in TestNG?
content.body: Use @Parameters with browser values defined in testng.xml.

content.heading: How do you pass multiple parameters?
content.body: Multiple parameters can be passed through testng.xml and injected into methods.

content.heading: What is suite execution?
content.body: Suite execution runs multiple test classes and test groups together using testng.xml.

content.heading: How does TestNG support Automation Frameworks?
content.body: TestNG provides execution control, reporting, data-driven testing, parallel execution, and integration capabilities required for scalable automation frameworks.

title: Playwright Interview Questions and Answers
summary: Comprehensive Playwright Interview Questions covering Playwright fundamentals, architecture, locators, browser contexts, waits, assertions, POM, API testing, authentication, network interception, parallel execution, debugging, and framework design.

content.heading: What is Playwright?
content.body: Playwright is an open-source browser automation framework used for end-to-end testing of modern web applications across multiple browsers.

content.heading: Who developed Playwright?
content.body: Playwright was developed by Microsoft.

content.heading: Which programming languages are supported by Playwright?
content.body: Playwright supports JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Python, and .NET.

content.heading: Which browsers are supported by Playwright?
content.body: Playwright supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit browsers.

content.heading: What are the advantages of Playwright?
content.body: Cross-browser support, auto-waiting, parallel execution, API testing, network interception, mobile emulation, and no WebDriver dependency.

content.heading: Does Playwright require WebDriver?
content.body: No. Playwright communicates directly with browser engines and does not require WebDriver.

content.heading: What is Playwright Test?
content.body: Playwright Test is the built-in test runner provided by Playwright for test execution, assertions, fixtures, reporting, and parallel execution.

content.heading: What is Browser in Playwright?
content.body: Browser represents an instance of Chromium, Firefox, or WebKit.

content.heading: What is Browser Context?
content.body: Browser Context is an isolated browser session with separate cookies, cache, and local storage.

content.heading: What is a Page in Playwright?
content.body: A Page represents a single browser tab within a Browser Context.

content.heading: What is Test Isolation?
content.body: Test Isolation ensures each test runs independently without sharing browser state, cookies, or storage.

content.heading: What is Headless Mode?
content.body: Headless Mode runs browser automation without displaying the browser UI.

content.heading: What is Headed Mode?
content.body: Headed Mode launches the browser with a visible user interface.

content.heading: What is Auto-Waiting in Playwright?
content.body: Auto-Waiting automatically waits for elements to be ready before performing actions.

content.heading: Why is Auto-Waiting important?
content.body: It reduces flaky tests and minimizes the need for explicit waits.

content.heading: What is Playwright Inspector?
content.body: Playwright Inspector is a debugging tool used to inspect and troubleshoot test execution.

content.heading: What is Trace Viewer?
content.body: Trace Viewer is a debugging tool that provides screenshots, DOM snapshots, network logs, and execution details.

content.heading: What is Codegen in Playwright?
content.body: Codegen is a Playwright utility that records user interactions and generates automation code.

content.heading: How do you start Codegen?
content.body: Use the Playwright codegen command.

content.heading: What is the Playwright configuration file?
content.body: playwright.config.js or playwright.config.ts is used to configure execution settings, browsers, reporters, retries, and environments.

content.heading: What are Locators in Playwright?
content.body: Locators are Playwright's recommended mechanism for finding and interacting with elements.

content.heading: Why are Locators preferred?
content.body: Locators provide auto-waiting, retryability, and better stability.

content.heading: What is locator()?
content.body: locator() creates a locator using CSS selectors, XPath, or other selector strategies.

content.heading: What is getByRole()?
content.body: getByRole() locates elements using accessibility roles and names.

content.heading: What is getByText()?
content.body: getByText() locates elements using visible text content.

content.heading: What is getByLabel()?
content.body: getByLabel() locates form elements associated with labels.

content.heading: What is getByPlaceholder()?
content.body: getByPlaceholder() locates input fields using placeholder text.

content.heading: What is getByTestId()?
content.body: getByTestId() locates elements using data-testid attributes.

content.heading: Which locator strategy is recommended?
content.body: getByRole() is generally recommended because it reflects user interactions and accessibility standards.

content.heading: What is the difference between Locator and ElementHandle?
content.body: Locator provides auto-waiting and retries, while ElementHandle points to a specific DOM element instance.

content.heading: What is Strict Mode?
content.body: Strict Mode ensures a locator resolves to exactly one element.

content.heading: What happens when multiple elements match a locator?
content.body: Playwright throws a strict mode violation error if a unique match is expected.

content.heading: How do you locate dynamic elements?
content.body: Use stable attributes, text, roles, data-testid, or partial attribute matching.

content.heading: How do you locate elements using CSS selectors?
content.body: CSS selectors can be passed to locator().

content.heading: How do you locate elements using XPath?
content.body: XPath expressions can be passed to locator().

content.heading: What is nth() locator?
content.body: nth() selects a specific element from multiple matching elements.

content.heading: What is first() locator?
content.body: first() selects the first matching element.

content.heading: What is last() locator?
content.body: last() selects the last matching element.

content.heading: How do you locate child elements?
content.body: Use chained locators.

content.heading: What are locator best practices?
content.body: Prefer user-facing locators, avoid fragile XPath expressions, and use stable attributes whenever possible.

content.heading: How do you navigate to a URL in Playwright?
content.body: Use the goto() method to navigate to a web page.

content.heading: How do you click an element in Playwright?
content.body: Use the click() method on a locator.

content.heading: How do you enter text into an input field?
content.body: Use the fill() method to enter text.

content.heading: What is the difference between fill() and type()?
content.body: fill() clears existing text and enters new text, while type() simulates keyboard typing character by character.

content.heading: How do you clear a text field?
content.body: Use fill('') or select existing text and delete it.

content.heading: How do you select a dropdown value?
content.body: Use selectOption() to select dropdown options.

content.heading: How do you select multiple dropdown values?
content.body: Pass an array of values to selectOption().

content.heading: How do you check a checkbox?
content.body: Use the check() method.

content.heading: How do you uncheck a checkbox?
content.body: Use the uncheck() method.

content.heading: How do you select a radio button?
content.body: Use click() or check() on the radio button element.

content.heading: How do you perform double click?
content.body: Use dblclick() method.

content.heading: How do you perform right click?
content.body: Use click() with button option set to right.

content.heading: How do you hover over an element?
content.body: Use hover() method.

content.heading: How do you drag and drop elements?
content.body: Use dragTo() method.

content.heading: How do you upload a file?
content.body: Use setInputFiles() method.

content.heading: How do you remove an uploaded file?
content.body: Pass an empty array to setInputFiles().

content.heading: How do you handle downloads?
content.body: Use page.waitForEvent('download') and save the file.

content.heading: How do you take a screenshot?
content.body: Use screenshot() method.

content.heading: How do you take an element screenshot?
content.body: Call screenshot() on a locator.

content.heading: How do you capture page title?
content.body: Use title() method.

content.heading: How do you capture current URL?
content.body: Use url() method.

content.heading: What are Assertions in Playwright?
content.body: Assertions validate expected application behavior and test outcomes.

content.heading: Which assertion library is used in Playwright?
content.body: Playwright provides a built-in expect assertion library.

content.heading: How do you validate page title?
content.body: Use expect() with toHaveTitle().

content.heading: How do you validate current URL?
content.body: Use expect() with toHaveURL().

content.heading: How do you validate element visibility?
content.body: Use toBeVisible() assertion.

content.heading: How do you validate element is hidden?
content.body: Use toBeHidden() assertion.

content.heading: How do you validate text content?
content.body: Use toHaveText() assertion.

content.heading: How do you validate partial text?
content.body: Use toContainText() assertion.

content.heading: How do you validate input value?
content.body: Use toHaveValue() assertion.

content.heading: How do you validate checkbox state?
content.body: Use toBeChecked() assertion.

content.heading: How do you validate element enabled state?
content.body: Use toBeEnabled() assertion.

content.heading: How do you validate element disabled state?
content.body: Use toBeDisabled() assertion.

content.heading: What is page.waitForTimeout()?
content.body: Explicit wait that pauses execution for a specified time. Generally not recommended.

content.heading: Why should hard waits be avoided?
content.body: They increase execution time and can create unstable tests.

content.heading: What is waitForSelector()?
content.body: Waits until an element appears in the DOM.

content.heading: What is waitForLoadState()?
content.body: Waits for a specific page loading state.

content.heading: What load states are supported?
content.body: load, domcontentloaded, and networkidle.

content.heading: What is Playwright's preferred waiting mechanism?
content.body: Auto-Waiting combined with assertions and locators.

content.heading: What are Playwright waiting best practices?
content.body: Use locators, assertions, and auto-waiting. Avoid hard-coded waits whenever possible.

content.heading: What is an IFrame?
content.body: An IFrame is an HTML element that embeds another webpage within the current page.

content.heading: How do you handle IFrames in Playwright?
content.body: Use frameLocator() or frame() methods to interact with elements inside frames.

content.heading: What is frameLocator()?
content.body: frameLocator() provides a reliable way to locate and interact with elements inside an iframe.

content.heading: How do you interact with an element inside an iframe?
content.body: Chain frameLocator() with locator methods.

content.heading: What is the difference between frame() and frameLocator()?
content.body: frame() returns a Frame object, while frameLocator() provides lazy evaluation and better auto-waiting support.

content.heading: How do you get all frames on a page?
content.body: Use frames() method.

content.heading: What is a Popup Window in Playwright?
content.body: A popup is a new browser page or tab opened from the current page.

content.heading: How do you handle multiple tabs?
content.body: Use waitForEvent('popup') to capture the new tab.

content.heading: How do you switch between tabs?
content.body: Store page references and perform actions on the required page object.

content.heading: How do you capture a newly opened page?
content.body: Listen for popup events before triggering the action.

content.heading: How do you handle JavaScript Alerts?
content.body: Use the dialog event listener.

content.heading: How do you accept an Alert?
content.body: Call accept() on the dialog object.

content.heading: How do you dismiss an Alert?
content.body: Call dismiss() on the dialog object.

content.heading: How do you handle Prompt dialogs?
content.body: Use accept() and pass the required input text.

content.heading: What mouse actions are supported in Playwright?
content.body: Click, Double Click, Right Click, Hover, Move, Drag and Drop.

content.heading: How do you move the mouse pointer?
content.body: Use mouse.move().

content.heading: How do you perform drag and drop using mouse APIs?
content.body: Use mouse.down(), mouse.move(), and mouse.up().

content.heading: What keyboard actions are supported in Playwright?
content.body: Press, Type, Key Combinations, Shortcuts, and Character Input.

content.heading: How do you press a keyboard key?
content.body: Use keyboard.press().

content.heading: How do you enter text using keyboard?
content.body: Use keyboard.type().

content.heading: How do you perform keyboard shortcuts?
content.body: Use key combinations such as Control+A or Control+C.

content.heading: What is Shadow DOM?
content.body: Shadow DOM encapsulates HTML and CSS inside a component, isolating it from the main document.

content.heading: Can Playwright handle Shadow DOM?
content.body: Yes. Playwright automatically pierces open shadow roots.

content.heading: How do you locate elements inside Shadow DOM?
content.body: Use normal locator strategies if the shadow root is open.

content.heading: What are Cookies in Playwright?
content.body: Cookies store user-specific information such as authentication and preferences.

content.heading: How do you retrieve cookies?
content.body: Use context.cookies().

content.heading: How do you add cookies?
content.body: Use addCookies() method.

content.heading: What is Local Storage?
content.body: Local Storage stores browser data persistently even after the browser is closed.

content.heading: What is Session Storage?
content.body: Session Storage stores browser data for the duration of the browser session.

content.heading: How do you access Local Storage?
content.body: Use page.evaluate() to execute JavaScript in the browser.

content.heading: What is Authentication State in Playwright?
content.body: Authentication State stores login information such as cookies and storage data.

content.heading: What is storageState()?
content.body: storageState() saves cookies and local storage data for reuse.

content.heading: Why is storageState used?
content.body: To avoid repeated login execution and speed up test runs.

content.heading: How do you reuse a saved authentication state?
content.body: Provide the storageState file while creating a browser context.

content.heading: What are the benefits of storage state?
content.body: Faster execution, reduced login dependency, and reusable authenticated sessions.

content.heading: What is Session Management in Playwright?
content.body: Session Management involves maintaining authenticated user states across test executions.

content.heading: How do you test multiple users simultaneously?
content.body: Create multiple browser contexts with separate authentication states.

content.heading: Why are Browser Contexts important for multi-user testing?
content.body: They provide isolated sessions without sharing cookies or storage.

content.heading: What are best practices for authentication handling?
content.body: Use storage state, avoid hardcoded credentials, isolate user sessions, and refresh authentication when required.

content.heading: What are Playwright best practices for session management?
content.body: Use isolated contexts, reusable storage states, proper cleanup, and environment-specific authentication configurations.

content.heading: What is API Testing in Playwright?
content.body: Playwright provides built-in API testing capabilities that allow sending HTTP requests and validating responses without using a browser UI.

content.heading: What is APIRequestContext?
content.body: APIRequestContext is Playwright's API client used to send HTTP requests and validate API responses.

content.heading: Why use API Testing in Playwright?
content.body: API Testing is faster, more stable, and helps validate backend functionality independently of the UI.

content.heading: How do you create an API request context?
content.body: Use request.newContext() to create a reusable API request context.

content.heading: How do you send a GET request?
content.body: Use the get() method of APIRequestContext.

content.heading: How do you send a POST request?
content.body: Use the post() method with request payload data.

content.heading: How do you send a PUT request?
content.body: Use the put() method to update resources.

content.heading: How do you send a PATCH request?
content.body: Use the patch() method for partial updates.

content.heading: How do you send a DELETE request?
content.body: Use the delete() method to remove resources.

content.heading: How do you validate API response status?
content.body: Use status() or Playwright assertions.

content.heading: How do you validate response body?
content.body: Convert the response to JSON and validate values.

content.heading: How do you validate response headers?
content.body: Retrieve headers using headers() method and validate expected values.

content.heading: How do you validate response time?
content.body: Capture timestamps before and after the request execution.

content.heading: How do you parse JSON responses?
content.body: Use response.json() method.

content.heading: How do you parse text responses?
content.body: Use response.text() method.

content.heading: How do you send query parameters?
content.body: Use the params option while making requests.

content.heading: How do you send request headers?
content.body: Use the headers option within the request configuration.

content.heading: How do you handle Bearer Token authentication?
content.body: Pass the Authorization header with Bearer token value.

content.heading: How do you handle Basic Authentication?
content.body: Provide username and password using HTTP credentials configuration.

content.heading: How do you reuse authentication in API tests?
content.body: Store authentication tokens and reuse them across requests.

content.heading: What is Network Interception?
content.body: Network Interception allows monitoring, modifying, blocking, or mocking network requests and responses.

content.heading: What is page.route()?
content.body: page.route() intercepts network requests and allows custom handling.

content.heading: Why is Network Interception useful?
content.body: It helps test application behavior without depending on real backend services.

content.heading: What is Request Mocking?
content.body: Request Mocking replaces actual API responses with predefined responses.

content.heading: How do you mock an API response?
content.body: Use route.fulfill() to return a custom response.

content.heading: How do you block network requests?
content.body: Use route.abort() to prevent requests from reaching the server.

content.heading: How do you continue intercepted requests?
content.body: Use route.continue() to allow the request to proceed.

content.heading: What is route.fulfill()?
content.body: route.fulfill() returns a mocked response to the application.

content.heading: What is route.abort()?
content.body: route.abort() cancels the intercepted network request.

content.heading: What is route.continue()?
content.body: route.continue() forwards the intercepted request to the server.

content.heading: How do you monitor network requests?
content.body: Use request and response event listeners.

content.heading: How do you monitor network responses?
content.body: Listen to response events.

content.heading: How do you validate API and UI together?
content.body: Create or validate data through APIs and verify the same data through the UI.

content.heading: What are the advantages of combining API and UI testing?
content.body: Faster setup, reduced dependencies, improved test stability, and end-to-end validation.

content.heading: Can Playwright test GraphQL APIs?
content.body: Yes. GraphQL requests can be sent using APIRequestContext like any HTTP request.

content.heading: How do you upload files using APIs?
content.body: Send multipart/form-data requests with file payloads.

content.heading: What is API chaining?
content.body: API chaining uses data from one response as input for subsequent requests.

content.heading: What are API testing best practices?
content.body: Validate status codes, response schema, headers, response time, authentication, and business rules.

content.heading: How do you design an API automation framework in Playwright?
content.body: Separate API clients, request models, test data, utilities, configuration, reporting, and assertions into reusable layers.

content.heading: What are the advantages of using Playwright for both UI and API testing?
content.body: A single framework can validate frontend and backend functionality, reducing tool dependency and simplifying automation maintenance.

content.heading: What is Page Object Model (POM)?
content.body: Page Object Model is a design pattern where page elements and actions are maintained in separate classes to improve maintainability and reusability.

content.heading: Why is POM used in Playwright?
content.body: POM reduces code duplication, improves readability, and simplifies maintenance when UI changes occur.

content.heading: What should be included in a Page Object class?
content.body: Locators, page actions, validations, and reusable page-specific methods.

content.heading: What is a Base Page?
content.body: A Base Page contains common methods and reusable functionality shared across page classes.

content.heading: What are the benefits of POM?
content.body: Reusability, maintainability, scalability, readability, and separation of concerns.

content.heading: What are Fixtures in Playwright?
content.body: Fixtures are reusable setup and teardown components that provide test dependencies.

content.heading: Why are Fixtures used?
content.body: Fixtures help avoid duplicate setup code and ensure consistent test initialization.

content.heading: What are built-in Fixtures in Playwright?
content.body: browser, context, page, request, browserName, and playwright.

content.heading: What is a Custom Fixture?
content.body: A Custom Fixture extends Playwright's built-in fixtures to provide project-specific functionality.

content.heading: How do you create a Custom Fixture?
content.body: Extend the base test object and define custom setup logic.

content.heading: What is Global Setup?
content.body: Global Setup executes once before all tests and is commonly used for authentication or environment initialization.

content.heading: What is Global Teardown?
content.body: Global Teardown executes after all tests complete and is used for cleanup activities.

content.heading: Why use Global Setup for authentication?
content.body: It allows authentication to be performed once and reused across multiple tests using storage state.

content.heading: What is storageState in framework design?
content.body: storageState stores cookies and browser storage information for authenticated sessions.

content.heading: How do you manage multiple user roles?
content.body: Create separate storage state files for each user role and load them during execution.

content.heading: What is Environment Configuration?
content.body: Environment Configuration manages environment-specific settings such as URLs, credentials, and execution parameters.

content.heading: How do you manage multiple environments?
content.body: Use configuration files, environment variables, or .env files.

content.heading: What is Test Data Management?
content.body: Test Data Management involves creating, storing, maintaining, and reusing test data during automation execution.

content.heading: What formats are commonly used for test data?
content.body: JSON, CSV, Excel, YAML, and database records.

content.heading: Why should test data be externalized?
content.body: It improves maintainability and supports data-driven testing.

content.heading: What is Parallel Execution?
content.body: Parallel Execution runs multiple tests simultaneously to reduce execution time.

content.heading: How does Playwright support Parallel Execution?
content.body: Playwright distributes tests across multiple worker processes automatically.

content.heading: What are Workers in Playwright?
content.body: Workers are independent processes that execute tests concurrently.

content.heading: How do you configure Workers?
content.body: Use the workers property in playwright.config.js.

content.heading: What are Retries in Playwright?
content.body: Retries automatically rerun failed tests based on configured retry counts.

content.heading: Why are Retries useful?
content.body: They help identify flaky tests and temporary environment failures.

content.heading: How do you configure Retries?
content.body: Use the retries property in Playwright configuration.

content.heading: What are Projects in Playwright?
content.body: Projects allow execution against multiple browsers, devices, or configurations from a single test suite.

content.heading: Why are Projects useful?
content.body: They simplify cross-browser and cross-device testing.

content.heading: What Reporters are available in Playwright?
content.body: HTML, List, JSON, JUnit, Dot, and custom reporters.

content.heading: What is HTML Reporter?
content.body: HTML Reporter generates interactive execution reports with test details and results.

content.heading: How does Playwright integrate with Jenkins?
content.body: Playwright tests can be executed through Jenkins pipelines and published with reports and artifacts.

content.heading: How does Playwright integrate with GitHub Actions?
content.body: Playwright can run automated test suites within GitHub Actions workflows.

content.heading: Why is CI/CD integration important?
content.body: It enables continuous testing and early defect detection during development.

content.heading: Can Playwright run inside Docker?
content.body: Yes. Playwright provides official Docker images for consistent execution environments.

content.heading: What debugging tools are available in Playwright?
content.body: Inspector, Trace Viewer, Screenshots, Videos, Logs, and Debug Mode.

content.heading: What is Debug Mode?
content.body: Debug Mode pauses execution and provides interactive debugging capabilities.

content.heading: What is Trace Viewer used for?
content.body: Trace Viewer helps analyze execution flow, network requests, screenshots, and failures.

content.heading: What are Playwright Framework Best Practices?
content.body: Use POM, reusable fixtures, stable locators, storage states, externalized test data, CI/CD integration, and proper reporting.

content.heading: What are the key benefits of Playwright for modern test automation?
content.body: Cross-browser support, API testing, auto-waiting, parallel execution, reliable locators, debugging tools, and framework scalability.

title: Generative AI & LLM Interview Questions and Answers
summary: Comprehensive Generative AI and LLM Interview Questions covering LLM concepts, Transformers, Prompt Engineering, RAG, Embeddings, Vector Databases, Fine-Tuning, AI Agents, LangChain, Security, and AI Safety.

content.heading: What is an LLM?
content.body: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a deep learning model trained on massive text datasets to understand, generate, and process human language. Examples include GPT, Claude, Gemini, and LLaMA models.

content.heading: What is Generative AI?
content.body: Generative AI is a type of artificial intelligence that can create content such as text, images, audio, code, and videos based on user prompts.

content.heading: What is a Transformer Model?
content.body: A Transformer is a neural network architecture that processes data using self-attention mechanisms instead of sequential processing. It was introduced in the paper "Attention Is All You Need".

content.heading: Why are Transformers important?
content.body: Transformers enable efficient parallel processing, better context understanding, and scalability for large language models.

content.heading: What is Prompt Engineering?
content.body: Prompt Engineering is the practice of designing prompts and instructions to improve the quality, accuracy, and relevance of LLM responses.

content.heading: Why is Prompt Engineering important?
content.body: Well-designed prompts improve response quality, reduce ambiguity, and help achieve desired outputs consistently.

content.heading: What is Tokenization?
content.body: Tokenization is the process of breaking text into smaller units called tokens so that language models can process input efficiently.

content.heading: What is a Token?
content.body: A Token is the smallest unit processed by an LLM. It may represent a word, part of a word, character, or symbol.

content.heading: What are Embeddings?
content.body: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of text that capture semantic meaning and relationships between words or documents.

content.heading: Why are Embeddings used?
content.body: Embeddings enable semantic search, similarity matching, clustering, recommendation systems, and retrieval operations.

content.heading: What is a Vector Database?
content.body: A Vector Database is a specialized database designed to store, index, and search vector embeddings efficiently.

content.heading: What are examples of Vector Databases?
content.body: Pinecone, FAISS, ChromaDB, Weaviate, Qdrant, and Milvus.

content.heading: What is Fine-Tuning?
content.body: Fine-Tuning is the process of training a pre-trained model on a custom dataset to improve performance for a specific domain or use case.

content.heading: When is Fine-Tuning used?
content.body: Fine-Tuning is used when domain-specific knowledge, terminology, or specialized behavior is required.

content.heading: What is RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)?
content.body: RAG is a technique where an LLM retrieves relevant information from external knowledge sources before generating responses.

content.heading: Why is RAG used?
content.body: RAG improves response accuracy, reduces hallucinations, and enables access to external knowledge sources.

content.heading: What is Hallucination in LLMs?
content.body: Hallucination occurs when an LLM generates incorrect, fabricated, or misleading information that appears plausible.

content.heading: How can Hallucinations be reduced?
content.body: Using RAG, fact verification, better prompts, domain knowledge sources, and validation mechanisms.

content.heading: What is Temperature in LLMs?
content.body: Temperature is a parameter that controls randomness in generated output. Lower values produce more deterministic responses, while higher values increase creativity.

content.heading: What is Context Window?
content.body: Context Window is the maximum number of tokens an LLM can process within a single interaction.

content.heading: What is Zero-Shot Prompting?
content.body: Zero-Shot Prompting provides instructions without examples and relies on the model's pre-trained knowledge.

content.heading: What is One-Shot Prompting?
content.body: One-Shot Prompting provides a single example to guide the model's response.

content.heading: What is Few-Shot Learning?
content.body: Few-Shot Learning provides a small number of examples in the prompt to help the model identify patterns and generate accurate responses.

content.heading: What is Chain-of-Thought Prompting?
content.body: Chain-of-Thought Prompting encourages the model to reason through problems step by step before producing an answer.

content.heading: What are AI Agents?
content.body: AI Agents are systems that use LLMs to plan tasks, make decisions, use tools, and execute workflows autonomously.

content.heading: What are the key components of an AI Agent?
content.body: Reasoning, memory, planning, tool usage, execution, and feedback mechanisms.

content.heading: What is LangChain?
content.body: LangChain is a framework used to build LLM-powered applications such as chatbots, AI agents, document search systems, and RAG solutions.

content.heading: Why is LangChain used?
content.body: LangChain simplifies integration of LLMs, prompts, memory, tools, vector databases, and workflows.

content.heading: What is Prompt Injection?
content.body: Prompt Injection is a security attack where malicious prompts attempt to override instructions or manipulate AI behavior.

content.heading: Why is Prompt Injection a concern?
content.body: It can expose sensitive information, bypass safeguards, or influence AI systems to perform unintended actions.

content.heading: What is RLHF?
content.body: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a training approach where human feedback is used to improve model quality, helpfulness, and safety.

content.heading: What are Guardrails in AI?
content.body: Guardrails are safety mechanisms that control model behavior and prevent harmful, unsafe, or policy-violating outputs.

content.heading: What is Function Calling in LLMs?
content.body: Function Calling allows LLMs to invoke external tools, APIs, or services to retrieve data or perform actions.

content.heading: Why is Function Calling useful?
content.body: It enables real-time information retrieval, workflow automation, calculations, and integration with external systems.

content.heading: What is an AI Copilot?
content.body: An AI Copilot is an intelligent assistant integrated into software applications to help users complete tasks such as coding, writing, analysis, and automation.

content.heading: What are examples of AI Copilots?
content.body: GitHub Copilot, Microsoft Copilot, Salesforce Einstein Copilot, and domain-specific AI assistants.

content.heading: What are common applications of Generative AI?
content.body: Chatbots, content generation, coding assistants, document summarization, translation, search systems, and virtual assistants.

content.heading: What is Semantic Search?
content.body: Semantic Search uses embeddings to find results based on meaning rather than exact keyword matching.

content.heading: What is AI Safety?
content.body: AI Safety focuses on ensuring AI systems operate reliably, ethically, securely, and within defined boundaries.

content.heading: What are Generative AI best practices?
content.body: Use clear prompts, validate outputs, implement guardrails, secure data access, reduce hallucinations, and monitor AI performance continuously.

title: CAD Design & 3DEXPERIENCE Interview Questions and Answers
summary: Comprehensive CAD, CATIA, and 3DEXPERIENCE Interview Questions covering Sketching, Constraints, Parametric Modeling, Part Design, Assembly Design, CAD Testing, Geometry Validation, Regeneration, Performance, and AI-assisted CAD validation.

content.heading: What are the types of CAD modeling?
content.body: Types of CAD modeling include 2D Drafting, 3D Solid Modeling, Surface Modeling, Parametric Modeling, and Direct (Non-Parametric) Modeling.

content.heading: What is Parametric Modeling?
